text: We present a case of robot-assisted complex prostatic urethrocutaneous fistula excision. Twenty-one-year-old male presented with complaints of passing urine and occasionally semen from the perianal wound, status post incision and drainage for perianal abscess which was done seven months ago.

The surgeon examines the perianal region, identifying a 0.5 cm external opening at the four o'clock position. During digital rectal examination, minimal thickening is palpated on the left side of the prostate, with no internal opening detected.

MRI imaging reveals a partially liquefied cystic structure communicating internally with the prostatic urethra and externally opening into the perianal region at the four o'clock position. Cystoscopy shows a diverticulum-like structure arising from the left side of the prostatic urethra.
The surgical team begins the posterior dissection between the bladder and rectum, carefully dividing the peritoneum using robotic assistance.
The video shows a dilated cystic structure replacing the prostate, with visible internal communication to the urethra and external communication to the perianal region.
During intraoperative cystoscopy, a large opening is observed at the seven o'clock position of the prostatic urethra. Upon further exploration, a large cystic sac is visualized.
The surgeon begins anterior dissection through the space of Retzius to access the cystic sac, which could not be fully dissected using the posterior approach.

The surgeon approaches the cystic sac from the left side of the bladder, incising the endopelvic fascia and dissecting the sac from surrounding structures. The sac visibly replaces the prostate and communicates internally with the prostatic urethra and externally with the perianal region, as confirmed by MRI findings.
Stay sutures are applied to improve the delineation of the cystic sac, followed by the division of the sac using surgical instruments.
During simultaneous cystoscopy, the surgeon identifies the fistulous opening into the urethra. The remnant sac is traced using stay sutures and then resected.

The surgical team uses electrocautery carefully near the neurovascular bundle to prevent injury. Simultaneously, another team injects methylene blue through the external fistula opening, and dissection begins from the perianal region.
The surgeon delineates the entire tract using methylene blue, followed by complete excision of the tract through a combined abdominal perineal approach.
The surgeon repairs the urethra using Vicryl 3-0 sutures. The defect in the pelvis is closed with StrataFix sutures, while the perineal wound is left open to heal by secondary intention.

Postoperatively, the patient was comfortable and discharged on day two. Histopathology report was suggestive of fibrocollagenous tissue lined by non-keratinizing stratified squamous epithelium with chronic granulation tissue with no evidence of malignancy or dysplasia.

Follies was removed after three weeks and patient was able to void normally. MRI pelvis was performed three months after surgery and showed edema at the site of resection which gradually resolved. Patient was able to void normally, ejaculate and is sexually active.
